draft.introduction: Surah Al-Baqarah (The Cow) is the longest chapter of the Quran, covering law, narrative, and theology. Its opening verses define the believers, the disbelievers, and the hypocrites—establishing the tripartite moral taxonomy of the Quran.

translation.text: Alif. Lam. Mim. This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of God—who believe in the unseen, establish prayer, and spend out of what We have provided for them, and who believe in what has been revealed to you and what was revealed before you, and of the Hereafter they are certain.
translation.original_text: الم ذٰلِكَ الْكِتَـبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۚ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

translator_notes.note: The disconnected letters (Alif Lam Mim) that open this and 28 other surahs remain among the Quran’s most debated features. No definitive explanation exists; they may be abbreviations, mystical symbols, or simply divine mysteries. Their presence underscores the Quran’s claim to transcend human comprehension.
